method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The island was first recorded in 1702 by the Spanish ship *Rosario* and was originally named Rosario Island in honor of the vessel.[3] It was later renamed to Nishinoshima, which translates to "western island".[2][3]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an active andesitic volcanic island located approximately 1,000 kilometers south of Tokyo and 130 kilometers west of Chichijima in the Ogasawara Island chain.[1][4][5] The island sits at the summit of a massive submarine volcano that is roughly 30 kilometers in diameter.[1] It is classified as a back-arc subduction volcano situated next to the Ogasawara Trough, a spread-center graben.[2] The region's volcanic activity is driven by the subduction of the Pacific Plate underneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4] Nishinoshima forms part of the Shichito-Iwojima Ridge, a volcanic front of the Izu-Bonin arc that runs parallel to the Ogasawara Trench.[4] The volcano features a main peak that emerges above sea level as the island, alongside two submarine peaks to the south and west, and a potential satellite cone on its northeastern flank.[4] 

The primary andesite magmas of Nishinoshima originate directly from the mantle through the shallow, hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite.[3] The silicon dioxide (SiO2) content of the volcano's ejecta ranges from 51.8 to 60.1 weight percent.[1] While the island's eruptions have historically produced andesite, an eruption in 2020 also ejected basalt, basaltic andesite, and dacite magmas.[3] Basalt magmas have likely been generated throughout the volcano's entire history, as the 2020 basalts are similar to older deposits found in surrounding knolls, but the eruption center for these basalts shifted to the island's summit in 2020.[3]

Volcanoes of this type are known for intermittent explosive activity, which is typically small but can occasionally reach a Volcanic Explosivity Index (VEI) of 4 or 5 after long periods of dormancy.[2] The first eruption in Nishinoshima's recorded history began in May 1973 as an undersea phreatomagmatic explosion about 400 meters east of the island's southern tip.[1] This activity featured five craters that produced lava flows and ejected volcanic blocks, eventually forming a new island by September 1973.[1] Although severe erosion destroyed much of the new volcanic edifices, the resulting deposits connected the new and old islands.[1]

A major series of eruptions began in November 2013, creating another new island that subsequently merged with the older landmass and destroyed nearly all of the island's vegetation.[2][5] The eruptive activity between 2013 and 2020 has been divided into four distinct episodes.[3] Episode 1 lasted for two years until November 2015, with an estimated average effusion rate of 200,000 cubic meters per day.[2][3] Following a period of rest, Episode 2 began in April 2017 and lasted for 120 days into August 2018, featuring a slightly lower effusion rate of 150,000 cubic meters per day.[2][3] Episode 3 was significantly shorter, lasting only a week, and showed a decrease in eruptive volume

==Ecology==
As a recently formed and continuously changing oceanic island, Nishinoshima serves as a real-time natural laboratory for scientists to study primary ecological succession and island colonization.[3] Researchers closely monitor the barren landmass to observe how species arrive and establish themselves, while also tracking the development of coral reefs and marine biodiversity in the newly formed coastal environments.[3] 

The island's frequent volcanic activity creates a volatile environment that repeatedly decimates its biological communities.[2] Prior to the recent eruptive phases that destroyed the island's vegetation, Nishinoshima supported plant life, including populations of the common purslane (*Portulaca oleracea*).[2][4] A 2019 eruption completely eradicated the remaining flora, rendering the local purslane extinct in the wild on the island.[2] Fortunately, researchers from Tokyo Metropolitan University were able to extract specimens of the plant just before the eruption, successfully preserving the local lineage in a laboratory.[2] 

The ongoing observation of Nishinoshima provides valuable insights into the evolutionary processes, adaptive radiation, and high rates of endemism that characterize the broader, highly isolated Ogasawara archipelago.[3][5]
